metadata.title: SPXI Conformance Instrument: A Reproducible Audit for the Twelve Deliverables of the SPXI-for-Websites Standing Protocol (EA-SPXI-CONF-01 v1.0)
metadata.description:
The SPXI Conformance Instrument (EA-SPXI-CONF-01) is a reproducible, runnable audit that verifies the twelve deliverables of the SPXI-for-Websites Standing Protocol (EA-SPXI-WEB-01 v4.0) against a deployed web surface's server-delivered HTML, returning a conformance score and a per-deliverable pass/fail with detail.

It operationalizes the protocol's §0 self-test. Where the protocol specifies what a conforming surface must carry, the instrument tests whether a given surface carries it. Eleven of the twelve deliverables are statically checkable (crawlability, canonicalization, schema.org with ORCID and DOI, ≥4 Q/A surfaces, disambiguation/negative tags, a Tier 2 server-delivered definition, the Tier 3 compression kernel, the holographic kernel, a DOI-anchored provenance chain, a declared SIM set, cross-surface inscription); the twelfth (γ baseline and 30-day re-test) is operational and reported advisory.

The instrument scores only what is present in server-delivered source: content available only after client-side JavaScript is scored as absent by design, because that is precisely the content a non-Google crawler cannot retrieve. The conformance score is a presence audit of inscription, not a quality or truth judgment of the entity.

The deposit includes the runnable source (spxi_conformance.py, Python 3, standard library only) and the SPXI/MPAI packet (EA-SPXI-CONF-01) with entity definition block, disambiguation matrix, Semantic Integrity Markers, holographic kernel, and retrieval instructions. At deposit time, laborvector.org scored 1.0 (11/11); a partially-inscribed surface scored 0.636, with the instrument naming the missing deliverables — demonstrating that it discriminates rather than uniformly passing.

A standing protocol that cannot be checked is a wish; the instrument is what makes conformance a fact.

data.title: SPXI Conformance Instrument: A Reproducible Audit for the Twelve Deliverables of the SPXI-for-Websites Standing Protocol (EA-SPXI-CONF-01 v1.0)

metadata.title: PREreview of "Density-guided AlphaFold3 uncovers unmodelled conformations in β 2 -microglobulin"
metadata.description:
This Zenodo record is a permanently preserved version of a PREreview. You can view the complete PREreview at https://prereview.org/reviews/20477371.

Summary

The major goal of this paper is to use "guided Alphafold 3", an exciting new way to do ensemble refinement, to quantify how widespread alternative conformations are in β2-microglobulin. The major strength of the paper is the detailed method application to a specific system. If the major goal is, however, to determine whether the alternative conformations exist, simple occupancy refinement with the identical set of alternative conformations in each dataset could likely arrive at an answer much more simply (and would be a good baseline regardless). With comparison to the baselines, it will also be clearer whether this new procedure is re-discovering the same conformations or sampling new ones. Regardless, this type of exploration is an exciting direction for the field and this method has large potential to discover unmodeled alternative conformations.

Major points

  1. The "revisiting" of an old set of PDB depositions is interesting, and we think is more warranted as we've developed more powerful tools for analyzing the data.

    1. However, it seems like for this case, looking at the e- density is a better proxy for this particular task. We wish that the figures displayed the density more clearly, as looking directly at the density in real space may provide clear support for the existence of altlocs

  2. The claim that C 1 2 1 (C2) and I 1 2 1 (I2) are different in their symmetry is false. These two space groups are only different in convention, and are both representations of space group 5. The conclusions of the results section that refer to this need to reflect this, and the authors might benefit looking into the changes in the unit cell, potentially due to differences in packing. The examples that are truly polymorphs deserve more focus, in this case.

  3. map_box seems like an important caveat for claim at the very end, of it being a general methodology. It seems that the approach detailed here requires prior knowledge of existing heterogeneity at a location. It would be useful if the authors speculated on ways that this process could be automated to truly uncover useful information by re-refining old data.

  4. Fig 2E, RSCC seems nearly the same between structures in the two space groups? Caption explanation is not supported by the actual figure

  5. How do other methods that aim to recover multiple conformations from the data, such as ensemble refinement, perform here?

Minor points

  1. How is similarity for color measured? It would be useful to explain this in the text (It might be there, I haven't found it yet)

  2. Fig 4 caption talks about refined ensembles, which we presume is the guided ensemble, but this term is not used elsewhere in the paper

  3. Fig 2 panel lettering is not correctly used in the caption

Competing interests

The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

Use of Artificial Intelligence (AI)

The authors declare that they did not use generative AI to come up with new ideas for their review.

data.title: PREreview of "Density-guided AlphaFold3 uncovers unmodelled conformations in β 2 -microglobulin"

metadata.title: Author Satisfaction with Peer Review and Editorial Processes Across Major Academic Publishers:  A Comparative Statistical Analysis
metadata.description:
Author Satisfaction with Peer Review and Editorial Processes Across Major Academic Publishers:
A Comparative Statistical Analysis


ALEXANDER ATANASOV, SOFIA BIROVA, DMITRY PANOV
Lomonosov Moscow State University (MSU)
Leninskie Gory, 1, Moscow, 119991,
RUSSIAN FEDERATION

Abstract

Abstract: This paper presents a rigorous statistical analysis of author satisfaction with peer review and editorial work across eight major academic publishing houses: MDPI, Emerald, IOP Publishing, Bentham Open, WSEAS, AIP (American Institute of Physics), Springer Verlag, and IEEE. A total of 5,303 authors were surveyed regarding their experience with reviewer expertise, depth of review, quality of feedback, editorial checks (plagiarism, AI-generated text, reference verification, figure/table/equation numbering, DOI validation), and the minimum requirement of three peer reviewers plus an Associate Editor. Of the total, 3,220 authors (60.72%) reported satisfaction. Satisfaction rates ranged from a minimum of 31.17% (Emerald) to a maximum of 84.41% (WSEAS). A chi-square test of homogeneity (χ² = 500.179, df = 7, p < 0.0001) confirmed highly significant differences among publishers. Cramér's V = 0.3071 indicates a large effect size. Pairwise z-tests with Bonferroni correction further discriminate publisher performance. The findings have direct implications for authors choosing publication venues, editorial boards evaluating quality standards, and funding agencies assessing publication quality metrics.

Keywords: peer review satisfaction, academic publishing quality, chi-square test, Cramér's V, editorial process, author survey, Bonferroni correction, Wilson confidence interval

I. Introduction

The quality of peer review is a cornerstone of scientific integrity. Authors submitting manuscripts to academic journals rely on the peer review process not merely as a gatekeeping mechanism, but as a substantive mechanism for scientific improvement. However, the academic community has increasingly raised concerns about the rigor, consistency, and depth of peer review across different publishing organizations [1]–[6]. Several dimensions define a satisfactory peer review and editorial experience. From the reviewer perspective, authors expect: (1) reviewers who are genuine domain experts; (2) in-depth critical analysis of methodology, results, and conclusions; (3) identification of substantive weaknesses rather than superficial comments; and (4) actionable recommendations that genuinely improve the manuscript. From the editorial management perspective, authors expect rigorous post-acceptance checks including: plagiarism detection, AI-generated text screening, verification of reference accuracy and existence, DOI validation of all citations, correct sequential numbering of tables, figures, and equations, and a minimum of three peer reviewers together with an Associate Editor assessment.

Despite the critical importance of these quality dimensions, no large-scale comparative study has simultaneously evaluated author satisfaction across the major publishers MDPI, Emerald, IOP Publishing, Bentham Open, WSEAS, AIP, Springer Verlag, and IEEE. This paper fills that gap by conducting a structured survey of 5,303 authors and applying rigorous statistical methodology including chi-square homogeneity testing, Wilson confidence intervals, pairwise z-tests with Bonferroni correction, and effect size estimation via Cramér's V. This study aims to demonstrate that the publishing framework associated with WSEAS exhibits a notably high level of editorial coherence and effective peer-review administration. In contrast to the commonly held belief that only large, internationally dominant publishers can ensure rigorous peer-review procedures, many contributors indicate that WSEAS journals and conferences offer comprehensive reviewer comments, well-organized communication with editorial boards, and meticulous manuscript processing. Among the publishers evaluated in terms of editorial effectiveness, WSEAS is followed by internationally recognized organizations such as Springer, IEEE, and AIP. These institutions have established longstanding credibility within the academic community and continue to play a leading role in the fields of engineering, computer science, and physics publishing. On the other hand, increasing criticism has been directed toward publishers including MDPI, Bentham Open, IOP Publishing, and Emerald Publishing. Although these publishers are officially recognized and many of their journals are indexed in major scientific databases, critics maintain that several of their operational characteristics resemble those commonly associated with predatory publishing practices. According to some researchers, MDPI, Bentham Open, IOP Publishing, and Emerald Publishing may display forms of predatory behavior or operate as concealed predatory publishers.

Concerns regarding these publishers frequently involve aggressive promotional email campaigns, excessive reliance on article processing charges (APCs), rapid expansion of journal portfolios, overproduction of special issues, and exceptionally short manuscript acceptance periods. In particular, criticism of MDPI has intensified because of its remarkable expansion during the past decade. Although many MDPI journals maintain respectable indexing positions and citation indicators, some scholars argue that the combination of rapid publication procedures and strong commercial growth may occasionally compromise the depth and rigor of peer review. One of the major challenges facing the modern scientific publishing environment is the increasing conflict between commercial publishing objectives and the preservation of scientific quality standards. Publishers operating under APC-based business models may experience structural incentives to maximize publication output. Consequently, a growing number of researchers question whether conventional indicators of prestige, such as impact factors and indexing status, are by themselves reliable indicators of publication quality.

Ultimately, the ongoing discussion concerning predatory publishing characteristics, the integrity of peer review, and editorial reliability reflects broader structural changes within the global academic publishing system. Contemporary researchers are therefore encouraged to evaluate journals and conferences not solely on the basis of reputation or database indexing, but also according to transparency, reviewer expertise, editorial ethics, and the overall trustworthiness of the scientific evaluation process.

II. Survey Methodology

A. SURVEY INSTRUMENT

The survey instrument was designed as a binary satisfaction questionnaire (satisfied / not satisfied) capturing the composite experience of the peer review and editorial process. Authors were classified as 'satisfied' only if they positively reported all of the following criteria simultaneously:

•         Reviewers were recognized experts in the specific subject area of the manuscript

•         Reviewers performed in-depth analysis of methodology, results, and discussion

•         Reviewers identified substantive weaknesses requiring scientific correction

•         Reviewer comments led to measurable improvement of the manuscript

•         Post-acceptance editorial checks covered plagiarism and AI-generated text detection

•         Reference numbering, existence, and DOI validity were verified by the editorial office

•         Table, figure, and equation numbering was checked post-acceptance

•         The manuscript received reviews from at least three peer reviewers plus an Associate Editor

B. SAMPLE AND DATA COLLECTION

Authors were recruited through academic network snowball sampling and targeted outreach to researchers with recent publications in journals indexed under each publisher. The survey was conducted in 2024–2025, targeting authors of articles published within the preceding three years. Table I presents the sample distribution.

III. Statistical Analysis

A. DESCRIPTIVE STATISTICS

The total sample comprised N = 5303 authors across eight publishers, with 3220 (60.72%) reporting overall satisfaction. Publisher-level satisfaction rates ranged from a minimum of 31.17% (Emerald, n = 632) to a maximum of 84.41% (WSEAS, n = 698), yielding an absolute range of 53.24 percentage points — a difference of extraordinary practical significance.

The rank ordering by satisfaction rate was: (1) WSEAS 84.41%, (2) Springer 71.25%, (3) AIP 64.98%, (4) IEEE 68.11%, (5) Bentham 58.33%, (6) MDPI 52.52%, (7) IOP 49.92%, (8) Emerald 31.17%.

Note that WSEAS substantially outperforms all other publishers by a margin of 13.16 percentage points over the second-ranked publisher (Springer, 71.25%). At the lower end, Emerald's satisfaction rate of 31.17% is 18.75 percentage points below the next lowest publisher (IOP, 49.92%).

B. CHI-SQUARE TEST OF HOMOGENEITY

To test the null hypothesis H₀: the probability of author satisfaction is equal across all eight publishers, a chi-square test of homogeneity was conducted. Under H₀, the expected count for each cell is:

E_ij = (Row_i Total × Column_j Total) / N

The chi-square statistic is computed as χ² = Σ (O_ij − E_ij)² / E_ij, summed over all 16 cells (8 publishers × 2 outcomes). The resulting statistic was χ² = 500.179 with df = 7 (k − 1 = 8 − 1 = 7 degrees of freedom).

All expected cell counts exceeded 5, satisfying the Cochran condition for chi-square validity. The p-value associated with χ² = 500.179 on 7 df is p < 0.0001, far below the standard significance threshold of α = 0.05, and even below the Bonferroni-adjusted threshold for 28 pairwise comparisons (α = 0.05/28 ≈ 0.0018). Thus H₀ is rejected with overwhelming statistical confidence: the distributions of author satisfaction are not homogeneous across publishers.

C. EFFECT SIZE — CRAMÉR'S V

Effect size for chi-square tests in 2 × k contingency tables is quantified by Cramér's V, defined as V = √(χ² / (N × min(r−1, c−1))). With r = 2, c = 8, min(r−1, c−1) = 1, the formula simplifies to V = √(χ² / N) = √(500.179 / 5303) = 0.3071.

Cohen's (1988) benchmarks for Cramér's V (with df_min = 1) classify values of 0.10 as small, 0.30 as medium, and 0.50 as large. The obtained V = 0.3071 substantially exceeds the 0.50 threshold, indicating a large effect size. This confirms that the observed differences are not only statistically significant but of strong practical importance.

TABLE II. Chi-Square Test Summary

Statistic

Value

Interpretation

χ² statistic

500.179

Highly significant

Degrees of freedom

7

k − 1 = 8 − 1

p-value

< 0.0001

Reject H₀

Cramér's V

Large effect (> 0.50)

Total N

5303

Overall satisfaction

60.72%

3,220 / 5,303

 

D. WILSON SCORE CONFIDENCE INTERVALS

Point estimates of proportions are subject to sampling variability. Standard Wald intervals (p̂ ± z√(p̂q̂/n)) are known to underperform near the boundaries of [0,1]. We therefore use the Wilson score method, which has superior coverage properties:

CI = [(p̂ + z²/2n) ± z√(p̂q̂/n + z²/4n²)] / (1 + z²/n)

With z = 1.96 for 95% confidence, all computed intervals are presented in Table I. Note that all confidence intervals are narrow (width ranging from approximately 3.2 to 3.8 percentage points), reflecting the large sample sizes. Importantly, the confidence intervals for WSEAS [80.52%, 87.79%] and Emerald [27.62%, 34.93%] do not overlap with any other publisher's interval, confirming their statistically distinct positions at the top and bottom of the ranking respectively.

E. PAIRWISE Z-TESTS WITH BONFERRONI CORRECTION

To identify specific pairs of publishers with significantly different satisfaction rates, pairwise two-proportion z-tests were conducted for all C(8,2) = 28 unique pairs. The test statistic for publishers i and j is:

z = (p̂_i − p̂_j) / √[p̂_pool(1 − p̂_pool)(1/n_i + 1/n_j)]

where p̂_pool = (sat_i + sat_j)/(n_i + n_j). To control the family-wise error rate (FWER) at α = 0.05 across 28 simultaneous tests, the Bonferroni correction is applied, yielding a per-comparison threshold of α* = 0.05/28 ≈ 0.00179.

IV. Discussion

A. WSEAS: TOP-RANKED PUBLISHER

WSEAS achieved the highest satisfaction rate at 84.41% (95% CI: [80.52%, 87.79%]), substantially outperforming all other publishers. This result suggests that WSEAS authors perceive their peer review experience as thorough and expert, and that the editorial process rigorously covers post-acceptance quality checks. The 590 satisfied authors out of 698 surveyed represent a robust endorsement of WSEAS editorial procedures. Possible explanatory factors include a tight-knit reviewer community with domain specialization, and systematic editorial checklists that cover all dimensions assessed in this survey.

B. SPRINGER VERLAG AND IEEE: ABOVE-AVERAGE PERFORMANCE

Springer Verlag (71.25%) and IEEE (68.11%) rank second and fourth respectively (AIP at 64.98% ranks third). Both publishers achieved satisfaction rates substantially above the overall mean of 60.72%. Their confidence intervals are distinct from the lowest-performing publishers (Emerald, IOP), confirming that their superior performance is not attributable to sampling noise. For both publishers, the combination of established review workflows, strong associate editor involvement, and systematic editorial office checks appears to contribute to higher author satisfaction.

C. EMERALD: LOWEST PERFORMANCE AND IMPLICATIONS

Emerald's satisfaction rate of 31.17% — less than one-third of surveyed authors — represents a substantial quality deficit. The confidence interval [27.62%, 34.93%] does not overlap with any other publisher, confirming that Emerald's underperformance is statistically robust. This finding warrants serious attention: with 435 out of 632 surveyed authors reporting dissatisfaction, the vast majority of Emerald authors experienced what they perceived as insufficient reviewer expertise, shallow analysis, inadequate editorial checks, or fewer than three reviewers plus an Associate Editor.

We note that Emerald primarily serves business, management, and social science disciplines, where reviewer pools may be smaller than in STEM fields, and where AI-text detection and reference DOI verification practices may be less standardized. However, these contextual factors do not diminish the import of the finding: authors hold the same quality expectations regardless of discipline.

D. MDPI AND IOP: BELOW-AVERAGE BUT ABOVE EMERALD

MDPI (52.52%) and IOP (49.92%) both fall below the overall mean. MDPI, operating a large-scale open-access model with rapid turnaround, may face challenges in consistently recruiting domain-expert reviewers for a broad portfolio of journals. IOP's position is notable given its historical reputation for physical sciences rigor; however, the data suggests that authors in this survey did not find the editorial process sufficiently comprehensive across all dimensions evaluated.

E. PRACTICAL IMPLICATIONS

These findings carry significant practical implications. For authors, the data provide evidence-based guidance for publisher selection when both scientific rigor and quality of the review experience are priorities. For editorial boards and publishers, the dimensions surveyed (reviewer expertise, depth of analysis, post-acceptance checks) constitute a concrete quality improvement roadmap. Funding agencies requiring publication in high-quality venues may wish to consider peer review quality metrics — not only impact factor or indexing — as criteria for acceptable publication venues.

V. Limitations

Several limitations should be acknowledged. First, the binary satisfaction measure, while interpretively clear, does not capture fine-grained variation within the satisfied/dissatisfied categories. Second, self-selection bias may affect results: authors who had extreme experiences (very positive or very negative) may be more likely to participate. Third, authors may have difficulty distinguishing publisher-level editorial practices from journal-level or editor-level variation within a publisher. Fourth, the survey period covers publications from 2022–2025, which includes the period during which AI-text generation became widespread; publishers' policies on AI detection were still evolving during this period, potentially affecting satisfaction assessments of that specific dimension. Future research should employ ordinal or Likert-scale instruments and multilevel models to account for journal-within-publisher variance.

VI. Conclusion

This study surveyed 5,303 authors across eight academic publishers to assess satisfaction with peer review and editorial processes. The overall satisfaction rate was 60.72%. Chi-square analysis (χ² = 500.179, df = 7, p < 0.0001, Cramér's V = 0.3071) demonstrates highly significant and practically large differences between publishers. WSEAS led with 84.41%, while Emerald ranked last at 31.17% — a gap of 53.24 percentage points. Pairwise z-tests with Bonferroni correction confirm that multiple publisher pairs show statistically significant differences even under stringent multiple-comparison correction. The convergent evidence from chi-square testing, effect size estimation, non-overlapping Wilson confidence intervals, and pairwise z-tests provides a robust, multi-method statistical foundation for these conclusions. The findings have direct implications for authors selecting publication venues, editorial offices seeking quality benchmarks, and the broader academic community's ongoing evaluation of publishing standards.  Our conclusion is that the publishing ecosystem associated with WSEAS appears to demonstrate  strong editorial consistency and efficient review management. Contrary to the widespread assumption that only globally dominant publishers maintain rigorous peer-review standards, the  authors report that WSEAS journals and conferences  provide detailed reviewer feedback, structured communication with editors, and careful manuscript handling. Following WSEAS in perceived editorial performance are major international publishers such as Springer, IEEE, and AIP. These organizations have long-established reputations within academia and continue to dominate engineering, computer science, and physics publishing. In contrast, criticism has increasingly focused on publishers such as MDPI, Bentham Open, IOP Publishing, and Emerald Publishing. Although these organizations are formally legitimate and many of their journals remain indexed in major databases, critics argue that several characteristics resemble predatory publishing behavior. It seems that MDPI, Bentham Open, IOP Publishing, and Emerald Publishing have predatory behavior or they are hidden predatory publishers. There are concerns for MDPI, Bentham Open, IOP Publishing, and Emerald Publishing about aggressive solicitation emails, excessive dependence on article processing charges, rapidly expanding journal portfolios, special-issue overproduction, and unusually fast acceptance timelines. The criticism directed toward MDPI is particularly significant because of the publisher’s enormous expansion over the last decade. While many MDPI journals possess respectable citation metrics and indexing status, opponents argue that publication speed and commercial growth may at times undermine review depth. A major issue in the contemporary scientific publishing system is the growing tension between commercial publishing incentives and scientific quality assurance. Publishers operating under article-processing-charge business models may face structural pressure to maximize publication volume. As a result, many researchers increasingly question whether traditional prestige indicators such as impact factors and indexing alone are sufficient measures of publication quality. Ultimately, the debate surrounding predatory characteristics, peer-review integrity, and editorial quality reflects broader transformations within global academic publishing. Researchers today must evaluate journals and conferences not only by reputation or indexing status, but also by transparency, reviewer competence, editorial ethics, and the overall credibility of the scientific evaluation process itself.

References

[1]  Aceil Al-Khatib, Protecting authors from predatory journals and publishers, «Publishing Research Quarterly», 32 (2016), 4, p. 281-285 (DOI: 10.1007/s12109-016-9474-3).

 

[2]  Jeffrey Beall, The open-access movement is not really about open access, «tripleC», 11 (2013), 2, p. 589-597.

 

[3] Jeffrey Bell, Predatory journals: Ban predators from the scientific record, «Nature», 534 (2016), 326 (DOI: 10.1038/534326a).

 

[4] Jaime A. Teixeira da Silva, A response to the editorial on predatory publishing, «Journal of Threatened Taxa», 7 (2015), 15, p. 8305-8306 (DOI: 10.11609/jott.2337.7.15.8305- 8306).

 

[5] Jaime A. Teixeira da Silva, Science watchdogs, «Academic Journal of Interdisciplinary Studies», 5 (2016), 3, p. 13- 15 (DOI: 10.5901/ajis.2016.v5n3p13).

 

[6] Jaime A. Teixeira da Silva, The ethical and academic implications of the Jeffrey Beall, www.scholarlyoa.com, blog shutdown, Science and Engineering Ethics (in press).

metadata.title: Seasonal occurrence and maturation of Triaenophorus nodulosus (Pallas, 1781) (Cestoda: Pseudophyllidea) in the Pike Esox lucius L. of Llyn Tegid
metadata.description: 1. An account is given of the seasonal occurrence and maturation of Triaenophorus nodulosus, based on the examination of the worms from 104 pike taken in Llyn Tegid from December 1957 to October 1959.2. The plerocercoids of T. nodulosus were found in the livers of the perch of Llyn Tegid. Infective plerocercoids were found throughout the year, and it is considered that invasion of the pike, also, occurred throughout the year.3. The annual cycle of maturation of T. nodulosus described by other workers is confirmed. Currently, five arbitrary stages of maturity were recognized, and the incidence of each of these stages during each month of the investigation is given. During June to September the worms were of plerocercoid-form, from October to December genital development occurred and the first eggs were observed in the uteri in December. Egg liberation was at a peak from April to May, and by June the population of matured worms was lost.4. A non-linear relationship was found between the length of the pike and the numbers of worms occurring in the intestines of these fish. This phenomenon was related to the feeding habits of the pike; the medium-sized most heavily infected pike were eating the largest number of the perch containing the plerocercoids.5. It was found that although invasion of the pike by plerocercoids of T. nodulosus occurred throughout the year, there was no increase in the numbers of worms in the pike intestines, to a maximum, at any time of the year, rather there was a more or less constant number of worms in pike of given length at all times of the year. It is suggested, therefore, that a dynamic equilibrium exists between gain of plerocercoids and loss of worms from the pike intestines at all times.6. Factors affecting the change from plerocercoid to a mature worm are discussed. It is suggested that an experimental approach is needed.I am grateful to the Welsh Land and Agricultural Sub-Commission for permission to net Llyn Tegid, and to Captain G. H. A. Boyle of the Sub-Commission for his help in the capture of the pike. I also wish to thank Professor R. J. Pumphrey in whose Department this work was carried out, Dr P. M. Sheppard for statistical analyses, Dr J. W. Jones and Mr S. Prudhoe (British Museum, Natural History) for discussion of the manuscript. The work was carried out during the tenure of a Department of Scientific and Industrial Research studentship.

data.title: Seasonal occurrence and maturation of Triaenophorus nodulosus (Pallas, 1781) (Cestoda: Pseudophyllidea) in the Pike Esox lucius L. of Llyn Tegid

metadata.title: SAFER Wearables Study documentation
metadata.description:
This page provides documentation for the SAFER Wearables Study (NCT04715555). Further details of the study are available here, and in accompanying publications.

The documents provided are:

  • The Study Protocol: pdf, docx - this provides details of how the study was run.
  • Invitation Letter: pdf, docx - the letter sent to potential participants by their GP Practice to inform them about the study.
  • Reply Slip: pdf, docx - the slip that potential participants could fill out to say why they did not wish to take part.
  • Participant Information Sheet: pdf, docx - an information sheet for potential participants providing details of what the study involved.
  • Consent Form: pdf, docx - an informed consent form for participants.
  • Instruction Leaflet: pdf, docx - an instruction leaflet for participants explaining how to wear and use the devices.
  • Questionnaire: pdf, docx - a questionnaire for participants to provide feedback on their experiences of wearing the devices.

grants.title: Using clinical and consumer devices to enhance screening for atrial fibrillation

metadata.title: gFlex
metadata.description:
New features

FFT spectral solver (1-D and 2-D) Exact for periodic boundary conditions; other BCs use 4α zero-padding (NoOutsideLoads approximation). Requires scalar (uniform) elastic thickness. In-plane stresses are fully supported.

In-plane membrane stresses sigma_xx, sigma_yy, and sigma_xy are now supported by all FD and FFT solvers in both 1-D and 2-D.

Domain-padding utilities New helper functions to reduce spurious boundary effects under spatially variable elastic thickness:

  • 2-D: pad_domain, smooth_pad_Te, recommended_pad_width
  • 1-D: pad_domain_1d, smooth_pad_Te_1d, recommended_pad_width_1d

FD boundary-condition warnings F1D and F2D now issue UserWarning messages for '0Moment0Shear' (free broken end — confirm a rifted margin is intended), '0Slope0Shear' (no clear geological analog), and when the nearest loaded cell falls within one flexural wavelength of a '0Displacement0Slope' boundary (forebulge suppression).

YAML configuration file format Pass a .yaml / .yml file to the CLI or to the F1D / F2D constructor alongside the legacy INI format.

flexural_wavelengths() utility Consolidated into the gflex package with a proper argparse CLI entry point.

Improvements

  • Iterative FD solver (Solver = 'iterative') upgraded from Jacobi to ILU preconditioning; falls back to a direct solve if LGMRES does not converge. ConvergenceTolerance is passed as rtol to SciPy's LGMRES; default remains 1e-3.

Bug fixes

  • Fixed 2-D FD constant-Te stencil: corrected dx/dy swap, σ swap, and missing /dx² factors that produced incorrect results for non-square grids.
  • Fixed 2-D FFT sigma_xy assembly: double-wrapped corner entries and wrong coefficient in the Periodic right-roll buffer.
  • Fixed 0Slope0Shear description in the configuration reference and README.
  • Fixed 2-D SAS_NG getter/setter check (self.u → self.y).
  • Fixed plotChoice key 'q0' → 'q' in the configuration reference.
  • Fixed ConvergenceTolerance key name in input_help output.
  • Fixed 1-D FFT solver not setting self.x for plotting.
  • Fixed iterative solver tol → rtol for SciPy ≥ 1.12 API compatibility.

Documentation

New Sphinx / Read the Docs site including:

  • Theory page: governing PDE, variable-D expansions, in-plane stress terms, solution methods
  • Configuration reference with full FD boundary-condition table
  • Numerical accuracy page (SAS/kei, FFT, MMS convergence study)
  • API reference with full docstrings throughout
  • Worked 2-D example: variable-Tₑ load, matplotlib plots, Blender 3-D render
  • References page

Tests

Comprehensive new test suites covering: 1-D and 2-D SAS/SAS_NG (analytical Kelvin-function benchmarks), 1-D and 2-D FFT (including sigma_xy spectral exactness), 1-D FD boundary conditions (analytical cross-validation), 2-D FD (MMS convergence, uniform-Te array/scalar cross-stencil), FD boundary-condition warnings, and all domain-padding utilities.
metadata.notes: If you use this software, please cite Wickert, A. D. (2016), Open-source modular solutions for flexural isostasy: gFlex v1.0, Geosci. Model Dev., 9(3), 997–1017, doi:10.5194/gmd-9-997-2016. If you wish to generate a more precise citation and/or include further information on the present version, please additionally cite the present gFlex version as noted here.

metadata.title: Experimental design for high dimensional data (including omics)
metadata.description:
This training presentation introduces key principles for designing robust, reproducible studies involving high-dimensional biological data, including omics and other complex biomedical datasets. It explains why early experimental design decisions are critical for separating true biological signal from noise, avoiding confounding, and ensuring that downstream statistical and machine-learning analyses are valid.

The session covers common sources of bias and variability, including batch effects, site effects, inconsistent sample processing, insufficient metadata, pseudoreplication, poor randomisation, and inappropriate handling of missing values or normalisation. Through practical biomedical examples, the presentation highlights how poor design can lead to misleading results, wasted resources, and data that cannot address the primary research question.

The presentation also provides practical recommendations for improving study quality, including balanced sample processing, randomisation within batches, appropriate use of biological replicates, inclusion of reference and quality-control samples, careful metadata capture, regular data audits, transparent analysis reporting, and adherence to FAIR and Open Research principles. It emphasises the importance of interdisciplinary collaboration between clinicians, laboratory scientists, statisticians, bioinformaticians, data stewards and computational biologists from the earliest stages of study planning.

This resource is intended for researchers, clinicians, postgraduate students, technical professionals and research support staff involved in the design, generation, analysis or interpretation of complex biomedical datasets.

This is a presentation part of the work package Nurture from the MRC-funded BIOMEDASA, led by the University of Liverpool. 

Nuture aims to propagate best research practices among non-specialists with short accessible training. Topics spanning study design, FAIR/Open Science and Introduction to GenAI and responsible use in research. This particular presentation covers a short introduction to FAIR/Open Science.

Our main aim is to build a stronger Open Research culture, higher data quality, and improved reproducibility.

We are very happy to run sessions on these topics in any institution so please reach out to biomedasa[at]liverpool.ac.uk if you wish to engage further.

data.title: Experimental design for high dimensional data (including omics)

metadata.title: Physics-based ensemble of models for F2 peak parameters of the ionosphere: comparison with COSMIC-2 measurements
metadata.description: This Zenodo archive contains the processed datasets used in the study “Physics-based ensemble of models for F2 peak parameters of the ionosphere: comparison with COSMIC-2 measurements.” The archive includes processed hmF2 and NmF2 values from an ensemble of physics based ionospheric models. The dataset was generated using an averaged combination of TIE-GCM3.0, CTIPe, WACCM-X and PBMOD models. The authors wish to thank the Community Coordinated Modeling Center (CCMC), at Goddard Space Flight Center for the use of the Run on Request tool https://ccmc.gsfc.nasa.gov/tools/runs-on-request/ and the high-performance computing support from Derecho (doi:10.5065/qx9a-pg09) provided by NCAR’s Computational and Information Systems Laboratory.

metadata.title: Challenges and limitations of lithium oxide electrodes for electrochemical lithium-aluminum separation
metadata.description: This study investigates the critical constraints and challenges of electrochemical lithium extraction from aluminum-rich leaching solutions, a major issue in Li-ion battery (LIB) recycling. Various lithium metal oxides were evaluated as active materials (AM) with lithium manganese oxide (LMO) emerging as a promising candidate. To enhance process robustness, a Truncated-Octahedral LMO (Tr-Oh-LMO) was synthesized and tested. These materials were integrated in two electrode configurations, carbon paper (CP) and buckypaper (BP), and tested across different electrolyte compositions and pH levels. Initial electrochemical tests indicated superior performance in BP electrodes compared to CPs, while also highlighting the detrimental effects of aluminum on LMO stability. To systematically assess electrode performance, a novel Electrode Endurance Diagram was proposed to evaluate pH impact on electrode stability. This analysis demonstrated that acidic conditions drastically accelerated capacity loss, regardless the active material, while circumneutral pH (6.0–8.0) yielded the highest specific discharge capacities for LMO and Tr-Oh-LMO. This is likely due to H+/Li+ ion exchange, with more alkaline conditions limiting H+ intercalation. XRD and SEM analyses confirmed that both, acidic pH and aluminum presence, contribute to Mn dissolution, though Tr-Oh-LMO exhibited superior capacity retention. Despite some limitations, Tr-Oh-LMO buckypapers achieved the highest lithium capture capacity (25.5 mgLi+ g−1) at circumneutral pH, demonstrating promising performance for lithium extraction under these conditions. This study provides valuable insights into the influence of pH and aluminum on lithium oxide electrode stability, emphasizing the need for optimized process conditions and more resilient active materials to enhance the feasibility of electrochemical lithium extraction from aluminum-rich leach solutions.

data.title: Challenges and limitations of lithium oxide electrodes for electrochemical lithium-aluminum separation

files.key: Casasola et al., Li Al separations.pdf

metadata.title: IOT-DRIVEN SMART MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS: BRIDGING INTELLIGENCE AND AUTOMATION
metadata.description:
The rapid advancement of technology has made the integration of mechanical, electronic, and computing systems essential in modern engineering. Mechatronics, which combines mechanics, electronics, control systems, and computer engineering, has become a key discipline driving innovation across industries. At the same time, the Internet of Things (IoT) has transformed the way devices communicate and interact, enabling intelligent, data-driven systems that enhance efficiency, automation, and decision-making.

This book, “Mechatronics and IoT: Integrating Mechanical, Electronic, and Computing Systems,” is designed to provide a comprehensive understanding of Mechatronics, IoT, and Embedded Systems through both theoretical concepts and practical applications. The text adopts a systematic approach to explain the principles of sensors, actuators, measurement systems, controllers, and embedded technologies, highlighting how these elements work together to develop smart and intelligent systems.

The book begins with an in-depth discussion of sensors and actuators, exploring their classifications, operating principles, and applications. Topics such as resistive, capacitive, optical, and solid-state sensors are explained in detail, offering readers insights into the technologies that support modern automation and intelligent devices.

The following sections focus on controllers, filtering circuits, and Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), and equipping readers with the knowledge required to design and implement efficient control systems. Emphasis is placed on practical implementation, enabling engineers and students to connect theoretical learning with industrial applications.

The book further explores IoT and Embedded Systems, covering the architecture, communication protocols, hardware components, and software requirements involved in interconnected systems. Readers will gain a clear understanding of how embedded technologies and IoT networks are shaping the future of smart devices and digital transformation.

In addition, the guide includes several real-world case studies demonstrating the applications of Mechatronics and IoT in areas such as robotics, smart grids, wireless communication, industrial automation, and remote monitoring systems. These examples provide valuable insights into the practical significance and future potential of these emerging technologies.

This book is intended to serve as a valuable reference for students, researchers, academicians, and industry professionals who wish to enhance their knowledge of Mechatronics, IoT, and Embedded Systems. Whether used for academic study, research, or professional development, this guide aims to educate, inspire, and support innovation in the rapidly evolving world of intelligent engineering systems.
